paragraphs.text: Leah bore Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah, and ceased to bear; but Rachel remained barren. And since she had heard from Jacob that Abraham had prayed for the barren Sarah and was heard, and that Isaac too had prayed for Rebekah and was likewise heard, she thought that her closed womb was not opened because Jacob did not pray for her. And so with anger and tears she says to her husband: “Give me children, or else I die” (Gen 30:1). She was angry, and so said “Give me children,” and not “Pray that children be given me.” Therefore Jacob instructs her that his fathers, though they had been heard by God, were not heard at once: Abraham after a hundred years, Isaac after twenty. But when Rachel heard that great patience was needed in her, that she might not faint with long waiting, she began to beg Jacob: “Go in unto my maid, and she shall bear upon my knees, and I shall obtain consolation from her” (Gen 30:3). Rachel said to him: “Abraham took Hagar and fulfilled the wish of Sarah, because he loved her; but thou art not persuaded by my words, because thou dost not love me.” Jacob, lest she should constantly repeat her unceasing request to give her children, agrees to take her maid. And of course, in order to make sons of bondwomen also heirs, together with the sons of free women.

So Jacob took Bilhah; she conceived and bore Dan and Naphtali. Then Leah, seeing that she had ceased to bear, began to urge Jacob to take her maid also. And when he said to Leah, “Thou hast consolation, because thou hast children,” she answered: “It is not just that thou shouldst subject one handmaid to another. If thou hast caused me offense by Rachel’s handmaid, then cause her also offense by mine.” Therefore, so as not to grieve Leah, to end the strife between the sisters, and to establish peace in the house, Jacob takes Zilpah. And she conceived and bore Gad and Asher.

After this Reuben “found mandrakes in the field, and brought them to Leah his mother” (Gen 30:14). The mandrakes, as is said, are a fragrant and tasty earth-fruit, like an apple. For these mandrakes Leah, mingling gladness with faith, brought Jacob in to her for the night. For it is written: “And God hearkened to Leah, and she conceived and bore… Issachar. And Leah said: God hath given me my hire, because I have given my maiden to my husband” (Gen 30:17–18). If it had not been by God’s will that Jacob took Zilpah, God would not have given Leah any reward for Zilpah. So Leah conceived and bore Issachar, then Zebulun, and Dinah their sister. “And God remembered Rachel, and she bore Joseph, and said: I see now that the Lord hath added to me, and not my husband” (Gen 30:22–24). After Joseph was born, Jacob said to Laban: “Give me my wives and my children, for whom I have served thee, and let me go.” But Laban, since he loved not Jacob but himself, says in reply: “I have learned by experience that the Lord hath blessed me for thy sake. Appoint thy wages, and I will give it” (Gen 30:26–28). Jacob agreed to the division, because he had not yet received from God permission to depart. But God, foreseeing that Laban would deprive of his wages the one to whom God had said, “I will go with thee, and I will bring thee back from thence,” enriched Jacob with the flocks of Laban without any wrong to Laban. For, as Laban himself learned by experience, God in his flocks multiplied the speckled and spotted sheep, that he might understand that God was with Jacob, and not wrong him any more.
